answer: Love at UCSB appears in everyday communities through friendship, mentorship, cultural belonging, and relationships. Instead of only seeing love from a romantic aspect, my field observations helped me understand love as emotional support and connections that students create through shared passions. I observed the Japanese Student Association fundraiser in front of the Arbor and an intramural soccer community at the UCSB soccer field. These moments showed students building connections through shared culture, teamwork, encouragement, and time spent together. In “Love Is Medicine for Fear,” Arthur C. Brooks argues that love can reduce fear and loneliness because supportive relationships make people feel emotionally secure. Diane Ackerman also explains in “The Brain on Love” that relationships shape emotional connection and reduce stress. Based on my observations and Unit 2 readings, love at UCSB is expressed through communities built around shared culture, interests, and teamwork, where students create emotional support, a sense of belonging, and connection through everyday interactions.
